flashcards.front: What is a proposition in propositional logic?
flashcards.back: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either definitely true or definitely false. It cannot be both true and false simultaneously.
An atomic proposition (or simple proposition) is a basic proposition that contains no logical connectives and represents a single factual claim. Example: 'It is raining'.
A compound proposition is formed by combining two or more atomic propositions using logical connectives. Example: 'It is raining AND it is cold'.
The negation (¬ or ~) is a unary connective that flips the truth value of a proposition. If P is true, ¬P is false; if P is false, ¬P is true.
Conjunction (∧) is true only when BOTH operands are true. It is false in all other cases.
Disjunction (∨) is true when AT LEAST ONE operand is true. It is false only when both operands are false.
P → Q (if P then Q) is false only when P is true and Q is false. It is true in all other cases (including when P is false).
P ↔ Q (P if and only if Q) is true when both P and Q have the same truth value (both true or both false).
A tautology is a compound proposition that is always true, regardless of the truth values of its component propositions. Example: P ∨ ¬P (Law of Excluded Middle).
A contradiction (or absurdity) is a compound proposition that is always false, regardless of the truth values of its component propositions. Example: P ∧ ¬P.
A contingency is a compound proposition whose truth value depends on the truth values of its component propositions. It is neither a tautology nor a contradiction.
Two propositions P and Q are logically equivalent (P ≡ Q) if they have identical truth values for all possible truth value combinations of their components.
Modus Ponens ( affirming the antecedent): If P → Q is true and P is true, then Q must be true. Form: [(P → Q) ∧ P] ⇒ Q
Modus Tollens (denying the consequent): If P → Q is true and Q is false, then P must be false. Form: [(P → Q) ∧ ¬Q] ⇒ ¬P
A truth table lists all possible combinations of truth values for the propositional variables in a proposition, showing the resulting truth value of the compound proposition for each combination.
